speaker: ALEX
text: You've reached a really important point. After a capstone, it can be tempting to think, okay, that was the assignment, I did it, time to move on. But what you practiced is bigger than a classroom exercise.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Because the habits matter, not just the one project?

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. In accessibility work, credibility comes from evidence. You practiced naming the user, describing the barrier, proposing a focused change, testing the result or clearly explaining it, and then inviting review. That is real project behavior.

speaker: JAMIE
text: I like that, because it makes accessibility feel concrete instead of vague. You're not just saying, this feels inaccessible. You're documenting who is affected, what is happening, what should change, and what happened after you checked.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. And GitHub places that kind of work inside a much larger mission. GitHub describes its mission as accelerating human progress through developer collaboration. Its accessibility program is framed around helping about 1.3 billion people with disabilities benefit from and contribute to that progress.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That framing is powerful. It says accessibility is not some side quest.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. It is not a niche afterthought. It shapes who can participate in software, who can become a contributor, and who gets to influence the tools the rest of the world uses.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So if a learner listening to this is blind, low vision, neurodivergent, mobility disabled, Deaf, hard of hearing, or has another disability, the message is: you are not outside the story of software development. You are part of it.

speaker: ALEX
text: Beautifully said.

speaker: ALEX
text: GitHub describes four strategic accessibility priorities, and they connect directly to the kind of work you've been practicing.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's walk through them one by one.

speaker: ALEX
text: First, employees with disabilities. GitHub says accessibility starts at home by supporting employees with disabilities. That includes accessibility training for all GitHub teams, improving internal tools and systems used by employees, supporting employee communities like NeuroCats and AccessCats, and providing accommodations and career growth opportunities.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the idea is that accessibility is not just something a company talks about for customers. It also has to show up in hiring, workplace systems, support, and advancement.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. If accessibility is only external, it's incomplete. An organization has to make it possible for disabled people to work there, grow there, and shape decisions from the inside.

speaker: ALEX
text: Second, developers with disabilities. GitHub says every developer should feel welcome and be able to use GitHub products, documentation, training, support, events, and community spaces.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That's broad on purpose, isn't it? Not just the website or one feature.

speaker: ALEX
text: Correct. The experience includes product interfaces, docs, learning materials, support channels, events, and community participation. GitHub also lists accessibility as a GitHub Fundamental. That means there are clear expectations for product teams, testing requirements and processes, accountability through GitHub Fundamentals scorecards, and regular audits and compliance tracking.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That word fundamental matters. It means accessibility is treated like a core quality expectation, not an optional polish step.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. And for learners, that should sound familiar. Good accessibility work is planned, tested, reviewed, and tracked.

speaker: ALEX
text: Third, customers building on GitHub. GitHub shares tools and practices to help customers meet their own accessibility goals.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What kinds of tools?

speaker: ALEX
text: Examples include a Figma annotation toolkit to support accessible design collaboration, the GitHub Accessibility Scanner action that can be added to a repository's continuous integration checks, guidance for accessibility-focused Copilot custom instructions so AI assistants can help with accessibility audits, and templates and patterns for accessible READMEs, contributing guides, and issue templates.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That's a useful list because it spans design, automation, AI-assisted review, and project documentation.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Accessibility is not one tool. It's a workflow. It can begin in design, continue in code review and CI, and show up in contribution docs that make projects more usable and welcoming.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And from an accessibility perspective, those templates matter a lot. An accessible README or contributing guide can lower the barrier to entry before someone ever opens an editor.

speaker: ALEX
text: Absolutely.

speaker: ALEX
text: Fourth, open source at scale. GitHub has pledged to help improve accessibility in open source by empowering people with disabilities to contribute to open source projects, increasing free and open source assistive technologies, increasing the accessibility of mainstream open source projects, and providing resources, tools, and guidance to maintainers.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That phrase, at scale, is doing a lot of work.

speaker: ALEX
text: It is. We're not only talking about one accessible repository. We're talking about habits, tools, and expectations that can spread across thousands of projects.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And maintainers are part of that equation too.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Contributors matter, and maintainers matter. If maintainers have better guidance, templates, tools, and review practices, accessibility improvements become easier to welcome, evaluate, and sustain.

speaker: JAMIE
text: I want to connect this back to the learner's capstone, because some people may still think, my project was small.

speaker: ALEX
text: A small project can still model excellent practice. Your capstone is an example of accessibility excellence because you named a real user need, described the barrier or opportunity clearly, proposed a focused change, tested the result or explained it carefully, and invited review and feedback.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So excellence here is not about flashy scope. It's about disciplined, evidence-based contribution.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. This is the same approach GitHub's accessibility program advocates. When you do this in open source after the workshop, you are participating in a larger movement toward accessible software.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That's encouraging. You do not have to fix everything everywhere. You can make one well-scoped, well-documented improvement.

speaker: ALEX
text: And that kind of improvement is often what real projects need.

speaker: ALEX
text: Why does all of this matter? Because the connection between your capstone skills and GitHub's accessibility priorities is direct.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's make that explicit.

speaker: ALEX
text: First, your evidence habits scale to thousands of projects. If you know how to identify a barrier, explain it clearly, propose a reasonable fix, and document results, you can bring that method into many repositories. Second, your contributions help normalize accessibility in open source. Every issue, pull request, review comment, test note, and documentation improvement helps move accessibility from exceptional to expected. Third, your presence matters. If you are a contributor with a disability, or a contributor with accessibility expertise, you change who has a voice in tool design. You broaden the perspective shaping software. Fourth, your skills show that accessibility is not separate from engineering. It is engineering.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That last point is worth sitting with. Accessibility is not an extra layer added after the real work. It is part of real work.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. It is part of quality, usability, maintainability, collaboration, and inclusion.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So where does a learner go next after finishing a workshop like this?

speaker: ALEX
text: You've learned the fundamentals, practiced with feedback, and completed a capstone that demonstrates accessibility-focused contribution. From here, reference lessons and advanced materials can help with optional tools and deeper workflows. But the real work starts after the workshop. The patterns you learned--finding issues, collaborating with others, reviewing code, building tools, improving documentation--are the same patterns you will use in real projects where accessibility matters every day.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That's a practical message. Not, congratulations, you are done. More like, congratulations, now you know how to participate.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Before we close, let's include the source-of-truth habit too. If someone wants current facts, where should they check?

speaker: ALEX
text: Use official references. GitHub Docs is the general home base for product and workflow documentation. The GitHub Changelog is useful when you need to track updates and evolving features. And GitHub Accessibility is the key source for accessibility commitments, priorities, and related resources.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And if someone wants to verify specific areas from what we discussed?

speaker: ALEX
text: For GitHub's strategic accessibility priorities, check GitHub Accessibility and GitHub Docs. For the practical picture of what excellence looks like in workflows, GitHub Docs and the GitHub Changelog are useful references. And for the broader why-this-matters and what-comes-next framing, GitHub Docs plus GitHub Accessibility are the right places to confirm current information.

speaker: JAMIE
text: I appreciate that. Accessibility work depends on evidence, and that includes checking authoritative sources.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Learn the principles, practice the workflow, and verify the details with current documentation.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the takeaway is: your accessibility contribution skills are real, they fit how GitHub thinks about inclusion at scale, and they belong in open source.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Your work matters, your perspective matters, and careful, evidence-based contributions can improve software for many more people than you may ever meet.
